quiz_questions.question_text: What is gravitational force?

options.text: The attractive force between objects that have mass
The repulsive force between charged particles
The force created by magnets on metal objects
The friction force between two sliding surfaces

quiz_questions.explanation: Gravitational force is the attractive force between any two objects that have mass. Every object with mass attracts every other object with mass. The strength depends on the masses and the distance between them.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the relationship between weight and mass?

options.text: Weight equals mass times velocity of object
Weight equals mass times gravitational acceleration
Weight and mass are exactly the same thing
Weight equals mass divided by the total volume

quiz_questions.explanation: Weight is the force of gravity on an object, calculated as W = mg, where m is mass and g is gravitational acceleration (9.8 m/s squared on Earth). Mass is how much matter an object has; weight is the gravitational force on it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the value of gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface?

options.text: 1.6 meters per second squared
3.7 meters per second squared
9.8 meters per second squared
25.0 meters per second squared

quiz_questions.explanation: On Earth's surface, the acceleration due to gravity is approximately 9.8 m/s squared. This means a falling object (ignoring air resistance) gains 9.8 m/s of speed every second it falls.

quiz_questions.question_text: What unit is weight measured in?

options.text: Kilograms for the weight
Meters per second for weight
Joules for the weight value
Newtons for the weight value

quiz_questions.explanation: Weight is a force and is measured in newtons (N). Mass is measured in kilograms (kg). A 1 kg object weighs approximately 9.8 N on Earth's surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you travel from Earth to the Moon, what happens to your mass and weight?

options.text: Both mass and weight stay exactly the same
Mass stays the same but weight decreases
Mass decreases but weight stays the same
Both mass and weight increase on the Moon

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter in an object and does not change with location. Weight depends on gravity, which is weaker on the Moon (about 1/6 of Earth's). So your mass stays the same but your weight decreases.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the weight of a 10 kg object on Earth (g = 9.8 m/s squared)?

options.text: 10 newtons on Earth
0.98 newtons on Earth
98 newtons on Earth
980 newtons on Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: Weight = mass x gravitational acceleration = 10 kg x 9.8 m/s squared = 98 N. The 10 kg object weighs 98 newtons on Earth's surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which has more gravitational pull: the Earth or the Moon?

options.text: The Moon has stronger gravitational pull
The Earth has stronger gravitational pull
They have exactly equal gravitational pull
Neither the Earth nor Moon has gravity

quiz_questions.explanation: Earth has a much larger mass than the Moon, so it has a stronger gravitational pull. Gravity on the Moon is about 1/6 of Earth's gravity. This is why astronauts could jump higher on the Moon.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to gravitational force as the distance between two objects increases?

options.text: Gravitational force increases with distance
Gravitational force stays the same always
Gravitational force decreases with distance
Gravitational force reverses its direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Gravitational force decreases as the distance between two objects increases. The farther apart the objects are, the weaker the gravitational attraction between them.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does gravity require contact between objects to act?

options.text: Yes, objects must touch for gravity to work
Yes, gravity only works through direct contact
No, gravity is a non-contact force acting at a distance
No, but gravity only works in outer space vacuum

quiz_questions.explanation: Gravity is a non-contact force, meaning it acts between objects without them touching. Earth pulls you downward even though you are not touching the center of the Earth. Gravity acts across empty space.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which direction does the force of gravity always act?

options.text: Upward, away from Earth's surface
Sideways, parallel to Earth's surface
Downward, toward the center of Earth
In random changing directions at all times

quiz_questions.explanation: On Earth, gravity always pulls objects downward toward the center of the planet. No matter where you are on Earth's surface, gravity points straight down toward the core.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is weight?

options.text: The amount of matter contained inside an object
The force of gravity acting on an object's mass
The volume that an object takes up in space
The density of an object at a given location

quiz_questions.explanation: Weight is the gravitational force acting on an object. It equals mass times gravitational acceleration (W = mg). Unlike mass, weight changes depending on the strength of gravity at the location.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is mass?

options.text: The gravitational force on an object at Earth's surface
The amount of space an object takes up in total
The amount of matter contained in an object
The speed at which an object falls toward the ground

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter in an object, measured in kilograms. Unlike weight, mass does not change with location. A 70 kg person has 70 kg of mass whether on Earth, the Moon, or in space.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'gravitational acceleration' mean?

options.text: The rate at which objects speed up due to gravity
The speed at which gravity waves travel through space
The distance gravity can reach from a large object
The total mass of a planet or star in the sky

quiz_questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration (g) is the rate at which objects accelerate when falling due to gravity. On Earth, g is approximately 9.8 m/s squared. On the Moon, it is about 1.6 m/s squared.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'free fall' mean?

options.text: Falling under gravity alone with no other forces acting
Falling at terminal velocity through the atmosphere
Floating weightlessly in outer space with no gravity
Falling and bouncing back up off the ground surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Free fall is when an object falls under the influence of gravity alone, with no air resistance or other forces. In true free fall, all objects accelerate at the same rate regardless of their mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'Newton's law of universal gravitation' mean?

options.text: Every object attracts every other object with a gravitational force
Only the Sun and planets can exert gravitational force on objects
Gravity only exists between the Earth and objects on its surface
Objects must be touching for gravitational force to exist here

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's law of universal gravitation states that every object with mass attracts every other object with mass. The force depends on both masses and the distance between them. This applies to all objects in the universe.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'g' represent in the equation W = mg?

options.text: The mass of the object in kilograms
The gravitational acceleration at that location
The weight of the object in newtons
The distance from the center of Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: In W = mg, 'g' is the gravitational acceleration at the location (9.8 m/s squared on Earth). 'W' is weight in newtons and 'm' is mass in kilograms. The formula shows weight is mass times gravity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by 'weightlessness'?

options.text: The sensation of having no apparent weight during free fall
A condition where an object has absolutely zero mass
A place where gravity does not exist at all anywhere
The state of an object that is completely at rest only

quiz_questions.explanation: Weightlessness is the sensation of having no apparent weight, experienced during free fall. Astronauts in orbit feel weightless because they are in continuous free fall around Earth, not because gravity is absent.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'inverse square law' mean for gravity?

options.text: Gravity gets stronger as distance increases
Gravity weakens with the square of the distance
Gravity stays constant at every distance apart
Gravity only acts between objects that touch

quiz_questions.explanation: The inverse square law means gravity weakens with the square of the distance. If you double the distance between objects, gravity becomes one-quarter as strong. Triple the distance, and it becomes one-ninth.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'gravitational field' mean?

options.text: The region around a mass where gravity can be felt
The surface of a planet where people can stand up
A type of shield that blocks gravity from acting
The core of a planet where all mass is located

quiz_questions.explanation: A gravitational field is the region around any object with mass where another object would experience a gravitational force. The field is strongest close to the mass and gets weaker with distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'force of gravity' mean?

options.text: The push force a surface exerts on an object
The attractive pull between objects with mass
The friction force between two sliding objects
The tension force in a stretched rope or cable

quiz_questions.explanation: The force of gravity is the attractive pull between any two objects that have mass. On Earth's surface, it is the force that pulls everything downward and gives objects their weight.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronaut has a mass of 80 kg. What is the astronaut's weight on Earth (g = 10 m/s squared for simplicity)?

options.text: 80 newtons on Earth surface
800 newtons on Earth surface
8 newtons on Earth surface
8000 newtons on Earth surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Weight = mass x g = 80 kg x 10 m/s squared = 800 N. The astronaut weighs 800 newtons on Earth.

quiz_questions.question_text: The same 80 kg astronaut goes to the Moon where g = 1.6 m/s squared. What is their weight on the Moon?

options.text: 800 newtons on the Moon
80 newtons on the Moon
128 newtons on the Moon
1.6 newtons on the Moon

quiz_questions.explanation: Weight = mass x g = 80 kg x 1.6 m/s squared = 128 N. The astronaut weighs only 128 N on the Moon versus 800 N on Earth, but their mass remains 80 kg in both places.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an elevator accelerating upward, does a bathroom scale show more or less than your actual weight?

options.text: The scale shows less than your actual weight
The scale shows exactly your actual normal weight
The scale shows zero regardless of acceleration
The scale shows more than your actual weight

quiz_questions.explanation: When an elevator accelerates upward, the floor pushes harder on you (greater normal force). The scale reads this normal force, which is greater than your actual weight. You feel heavier.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5 kg watermelon and a 0.5 kg apple are dropped from the same height (ignore air resistance). Which hits the ground first?

options.text: The watermelon hits the ground first
The apple hits the ground first overall
They hit the ground at the same time
Neither hits the ground without air

quiz_questions.explanation: Ignoring air resistance, all objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass. Both accelerate at 9.8 m/s squared and hit the ground at the same time. Galileo famously demonstrated this principle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do astronauts float inside the International Space Station?

options.text: Because they are in continuous free fall orbiting Earth
Because there is absolutely no gravity in outer space
Because the space station creates anti-gravity fields
Because they become massless when leaving the Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: Astronauts float because they and the space station are in continuous free fall around Earth. Gravity is still present (about 90% of surface gravity), but everything falls together, creating the sensation of weightlessness.

quiz_questions.question_text: A planet has twice Earth's mass but the same size. How would your weight compare to Earth?

options.text: Your weight would be about the same
Your weight would be about double
Your weight would be about half as much
Your weight would be zero on this planet

quiz_questions.explanation: A planet with twice Earth's mass (same size) would have about twice the gravitational acceleration. Since W = mg, your weight would be approximately double what it is on Earth.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures their weight as 600 N on Earth. What is their approximate mass?

options.text: About 61 kilograms of mass
About 600 kilograms of mass
About 6000 kilograms of mass
About 6 kilograms of total mass

quiz_questions.explanation: Using W = mg: m = W/g = 600 N / 9.8 m/s squared is approximately 61 kg. The student has a mass of about 61 kilograms.

quiz_questions.question_text: You hold a ball in your hand then let go. What force causes it to fall?

options.text: Air resistance pushes the ball downward
The normal force from your hand is released
Gravity pulls the ball toward the Earth
Friction between ball and air pushes it down

quiz_questions.explanation: When you release the ball, gravity (which was always acting) is no longer balanced by your hand's support. The gravitational force pulls the ball downward toward Earth, causing it to fall.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two objects are 10 meters apart. If you move them to 20 meters apart, what happens to the gravitational force?

options.text: It decreases to one-quarter of the original force
It decreases to one-half of the original force
It stays exactly the same as the original force
It increases to double the original force value

quiz_questions.explanation: The inverse square law says gravity decreases with the square of distance. Doubling the distance (from 10 m to 20 m) reduces gravity to 1/2 squared = 1/4 of the original force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bathroom scale reads 0 N while you stand on it in a falling elevator. What is happening?

options.text: You have become completely massless inside the elevator
Gravity has stopped acting on you inside this elevator
You and the scale are in free fall together at the same rate
The scale is broken and needs to be replaced right away

quiz_questions.explanation: In a freely falling elevator, you and the scale fall at the same rate. There is no contact force between you and the scale, so it reads zero. You still have weight, but you experience apparent weightlessness.
